questions.title: Validate Email Format for User Registration
questions.description: You are building a user registration system for a web application. As part of the registration process, you need to validate email addresses to ensure they follow a proper format before storing them in the database. A valid email address must contain exactly one '@' symbol, have a local part before the '@' (at least 1 character, alphanumeric with allowed special characters . _ % + -), and a domain part after the '@' (at least 1 character, alphanumeric with allowed dots and hyphens, must contain at least one dot). The domain extension must be at least 2 characters long. Write a function that takes an email string and returns True if it's valid, False otherwise.

examples.explanation: Valid email with proper format: local part 'user', '@' symbol, domain 'example.com' with valid extension
Missing '@' symbol - not a valid email format

test_cases.description: Valid email with dot in local part
Valid email with plus sign and multi-part domain
Missing domain extension (no dot in domain)
Domain extension too short (less than 2 chars)

hints: Check for exactly one '@' symbol in the email
Split the email at '@' and validate both parts separately
The domain must contain at least one dot and have a valid extension
Consider using regex for more robust validation
questions.solution: Split the email at '@' and validate both parts. Check that there's exactly one '@', the local part is non-empty with valid characters, and the domain has at least one dot with a valid extension of 2+ characters.

questions.title: Parse CSV Line for Data Import
questions.description: You are working on a data import tool that reads CSV files and processes each line. Your task is to implement a function that parses a single CSV line into a list of values. The CSV format follows standard rules: fields are separated by commas, fields containing commas should be enclosed in double quotes, double quotes within quoted fields are escaped by doubling them (e.g., "" becomes "). Empty fields should be preserved as empty strings. The function should handle edge cases like trailing commas, quoted empty strings, and escaped quotes properly.

examples.explanation: Simple CSV line with three fields separated by commas
Quoted field containing a comma is preserved as a single value

test_cases.description: Empty line returns single empty field

hints: Track whether you're inside a quoted field with a boolean flag
Handle escaped quotes ("") by checking for consecutive quotes
Empty fields between commas should still be added as empty strings
Consider edge cases like empty input and trailing commas
questions.solution: Iterate through each character, tracking whether you're inside quotes. When inside quotes, treat commas as regular characters. Handle escaped quotes by checking for consecutive double quotes. Build each field character by character.
constraints: Input is a single line (no newlines)
Quotes are only used for escaping commas
Escaped quotes appear as double quotes within quoted fields

questions.title: Format Phone Number for Contact Management
questions.description: You are building a contact management system that needs to standardize phone numbers for storage and display. The system receives phone numbers in various formats and needs to convert them to a standard format. Write a function that takes a phone number string (which may contain digits, spaces, dashes, parentheses, and an optional leading '+' for country code) and returns it formatted as '(XXX) XXX-XXXX' for 10-digit numbers or '+X (XXX) XXX-XXXX' for numbers with a country code. If the number is invalid (less than 10 digits after stripping non-digit characters), return 'Invalid'.

examples.explanation: 10-digit number formatted with parentheses and dash
Number with country code preserved and formatted

test_cases.description: Number with country code and spaces

hints: First extract only the digits from the input string
Check if the number has a country code (more than 10 digits)
Format the last 10 digits as the main number
Any digits beyond 10 are treated as country code
questions.solution: Extract all digits from the input. If less than 10 digits, return 'Invalid'. For exactly 10 digits, format as '(XXX) XXX-XXXX'. For more digits, treat the extra leading digits as country code and format accordingly.
constraints: Input may contain digits, spaces, dashes, parentheses, and '+'
Minimum 10 digits required for valid number

questions.title: Calculate Shopping Cart Total with Tax
questions.description: You are building an e-commerce platform's checkout system. Implement a function that calculates the total cost of items in a shopping cart, including applicable taxes and discounts. The function receives a list of items (each with name, price, and quantity), a tax rate percentage, and an optional discount percentage. Calculate the subtotal, apply the discount first, then add tax to get the final total. Round the final result to 2 decimal places. Return a dictionary with subtotal, discount_amount, tax_amount, and total.

examples.explanation: Subtotal: 1.50 * 3 = 4.50, Discount: 10% of 4.50 = 0.45, After discount: 4.05, Tax: 8.5% of 4.05 = 0.34, Total: 4.39

test_cases.description: Single item with tax and discount

hints: Calculate subtotal by summing price * quantity for each item
Apply discount before calculating tax
Use round(value, 2) to ensure 2 decimal places
Handle edge cases like empty cart and zero values
questions.solution: 1. Calculate subtotal by iterating through items and summing price * quantity. 2. Calculate discount_amount as subtotal * (discount / 100). 3. Calculate price_after_discount = subtotal - discount_amount. 4. Calculate tax_amount as price_after_discount * (tax_rate / 100). 5. Total = price_after_discount + tax_amount. Round all values to 2 decimal places.

questions.title: Find Duplicate Transactions in Financial Data
questions.description: You are working on a fraud detection system for a financial institution. The system needs to identify potentially duplicate transactions that might indicate errors or fraudulent activity. A duplicate transaction is defined as two or more transactions with the same amount, same merchant name (case-insensitive comparison), and timestamps within 60 seconds of each other. Write a function that takes a list of transactions (each with id, amount, merchant, and timestamp in ISO format) and returns groups of duplicate transaction IDs. Each group should contain at least 2 transaction IDs that are duplicates of each other.

starter.python:
def find_duplicate_transactions(transactions: list) -> list:
    # transactions = [{"id": str, "amount": float, "merchant": str, "timestamp": str}, ...]
    # Return list of lists, each containing duplicate transaction IDs
    return []

examples.explanation: Both transactions have same amount (50.00), same merchant (Amazon/amazon - case insensitive), and timestamps within 60 seconds

test_cases.description: Not duplicates - more than 60 seconds apart

hints: Parse timestamps to compare time differences
Use case-insensitive comparison for merchant names
Group transactions by (amount, merchant) first for efficiency
Check if timestamps are within 60 seconds for potential duplicates
questions.solution: 1. Parse all timestamps to datetime objects. 2. Group transactions by (amount, merchant.lower()). 3. For each group, sort by timestamp. 4. Find chains of transactions where each consecutive pair is within 60 seconds. 5. Return groups with at least 2 transactions.

questions.title: Merge User Profiles from Multiple Sources
questions.description: You are building a customer data platform that consolidates user information from multiple sources (web app, mobile app, CRM system). Each source provides partial user data, and you need to merge them into a complete user profile. When merging, follow these rules: 1) For conflicting values, prefer the most recent data (based on 'updated_at' timestamp), 2) For lists (like tags), merge and deduplicate, 3) For nested objects, merge recursively, 4) Missing values should be filled from any available source. Write a function that takes a list of user profiles and returns a single merged profile.

examples.explanation: Second profile is more recent, so its values take precedence

hints: Sort profiles by updated_at timestamp before merging
Use a recursive merge function for nested objects
For lists, use set operations to deduplicate
Handle null/missing values by keeping non-null values
questions.solution: 1. Sort profiles by updated_at timestamp. 2. Start with the oldest profile as base. 3. For each subsequent profile, merge fields: simple values take newer, lists are merged and deduplicated, objects are recursively merged. 4. Return the final merged profile.
constraints: All profiles have the same id
Nested objects can be arbitrarily deep

questions.title: Validate Password Strength for Security
questions.description: You are implementing a password validation system for a secure application. The password policy requires: minimum 8 characters, at least one uppercase letter, at least one lowercase letter, at least one digit, at least one special character from !@#$%^&*()_+-=[], and no more than 2 consecutive identical characters (e.g., 'aaa' is invalid). Write a function that validates a password and returns a dictionary with 'valid' (boolean) and 'errors' (list of error messages for failed requirements).

examples.explanation: Password meets all requirements: 11 chars, uppercase S, lowercase, digit 1, special char !, no consecutive identical chars

examples.output: {"valid": false, "errors": ["Password must be at least 8 characters", "Password must contain at least one uppercase letter", "Password must contain at least one digit", "Password must contain at least one special character"]}

test_cases.expected_output: {"valid": false, "errors": ["Password must be at least 8 characters", "Password must contain at least one uppercase letter", "Password must contain at least one digit", "Password must contain at least one special character"]}
{"valid": false, "errors": ["Password must contain at least one special character"]}
{"valid": false, "errors": ["Password must contain at least one uppercase letter"]}
{"valid": false, "errors": ["Password must contain at least one lowercase letter"]}
{"valid": false, "errors": ["Password must contain at least one digit"]}
{"valid": false, "errors": ["Password must be at least 8 characters"]}
{"valid": false, "errors": ["Password must not have more than 2 consecutive identical characters"]}

{"valid": false, "errors": ["Password must be at least 8 characters", "Password must contain at least one uppercase letter", "Password must contain at least one lowercase letter", "Password must contain at least one digit", "Password must contain at least one special character"]}
{"valid": false, "errors": ["Password must not have more than 2 consecutive identical characters"]}

hints: Check each requirement separately and collect all errors
Use regex or character class checks for uppercase, lowercase, digit, special
Iterate through password to check consecutive identical characters
Return all errors, not just the first one
questions.solution: 1. Check length >= 8. 2. Check for at least one uppercase using isupper() or regex. 3. Check for at least one lowercase using islower() or regex. 4. Check for at least one digit using isdigit() or regex. 5. Check for at least one special character from the allowed set. 6. Check for no more than 2 consecutive identical characters by iterating. 7. Collect all errors and return validity status.

questions.title: Find Available Meeting Time Slots
questions.description: You are building a calendar application that needs to find available meeting time slots for a group of participants. Given a list of participants with their existing meetings (each meeting has start and end time in 'HH:MM' format), a meeting duration in minutes, and a time range to search within, find all available time slots where all participants are free. The available slots should be at least as long as the required meeting duration. Return slots as a list of {start, end} objects.

examples.explanation: Participant is busy 9-10, so available slots are 10-12 (2 hours, enough for 60 min meeting)

test_cases.description: Gap too short for meeting duration

hints: Convert time strings to minutes for easier calculation
Merge all meetings from all participants into a single timeline
Find gaps between merged meetings that are >= duration
Convert back to HH:MM format for output
questions.solution: 1. Convert all times to minutes from midnight. 2. Collect all meetings from all participants. 3. Sort and merge overlapping meetings. 4. Find gaps between merged meetings that are >= duration. 5. Convert gap times back to HH:MM format.
constraints: Meeting times are non-overlapping per participant

questions.title: Parse and Filter Server Log Entries
questions.description: You are building a log analysis tool for a web server. The server logs are in a specific format: '[TIMESTAMP] [LEVEL] [SOURCE] - Message'. TIMESTAMP is in ISO format, LEVEL is one of DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL, SOURCE is the service name. Write a function that parses log entries and filters them by level (minimum level threshold), time range (start and end), and source (optional). Return a list of parsed log entries with timestamp, level, source, and message fields, sorted by timestamp.

examples.explanation: Only ERROR log passes the WARN threshold filter

test_cases.description: All log levels - filter WARN and above

test_cases.input:
["[2024-01-15T10:30:00Z] [INFO] [api] - Request with multiple words in message"]
INFO
{"start": "2024-01-15T00:00:00Z", "end": "2024-01-15T23:59:59Z"}
null

hints: Use regex to parse the log format: \[(.*?)\] \[(.*?)\] \[(.*?)\] - (.*)
Create a level hierarchy: DEBUG < INFO < WARN < ERROR < FATAL
Parse timestamps for time range comparison
Sort results by timestamp before returning
questions.solution: 1. Parse each log line using regex to extract timestamp, level, source, message. 2. Filter by level threshold using a level hierarchy. 3. Filter by time range by comparing timestamps. 4. Filter by source if provided. 5. Sort remaining entries by timestamp.
constraints: Levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL

questions.title: Compress String Data for Storage Optimization
questions.description: You are working on a data storage optimization system that needs to compress repetitive string data. Implement a run-length encoding compression algorithm. The compression format uses the count followed by the character for runs of 2 or more identical characters (e.g., 'aaabb' becomes '3a2b'). Single characters are left as-is without a count. Write both compress and decompress functions. The compress function should only compress if the result is shorter than the original. Handle edge cases like empty strings and strings with no repeated characters.

examples.explanation: Three 'a's followed by two 'b's - compressed to '3a2b' (5 chars -> 4 chars)
No repeated characters - compression would make it longer, return original

hints: Iterate through string, counting consecutive identical characters
Only add count if run length >= 2
Compare compressed length with original before returning
For decompression, parse digits followed by character
questions.solution: Compression: Iterate through string, count consecutive identical characters. For runs of 2+, output count+char. For singles, just output char. Return compressed only if shorter. Decompression: Parse digits followed by character, repeat character that many times.
constraints: Input contains only printable ASCII characters
Numbers in original string need special handling
Compression only applied if result is shorter
Run length can be any positive integer
